title: Merge sort and quicksort

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Merge sort and quicksort; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Merge sort and quicksort
sources.claim: Merge sort divides and merges with predictable O(n log n) time and extra storage, while quicksort partitions and has input-dependent recursion.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Merge sort divides and merges with predictable O(n log n) time and extra storage, while quicksort partitions and has input-dependent recursion.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A merge step combines two sorted half-ranges while preserving their combined order invariant. Your goal is to implement and test merge sort and partition logic and compare their space and case behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Arrays, strings and matrices; Time, space and asymptotic growth; Selection and insertion sort; Divide-and-conquer strategy. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Merge sort divides and merges with predictable O(n log n) time and extra storage, while quicksort partitions and has input-dependent recursion. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Pivot policy, stability, stack depth, in-place meaning, and worst-case behavior affect selection. The draft therefore rejects: Quicksort is always O(n log n) regardless of pivot and input.

includes: Apply divide-and-conquer to stable merging and partition-based sorting with worst-case awareness.
Implement and test merge sort and partition logic and compare their space and case behavior.

In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.
excludes: Pivot policy, stability, stack depth, in-place meaning, and worst-case behavior affect selection.

outcomes: Implement and test merge sort and partition logic and compare their space and case behavior.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Quicksort has quadratic worst cases; pivot strategy and introspective fallback manage that risk.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Pivot policy, stability, stack depth, in-place meaning, and worst-case behavior affect selection.
